critic_raw:
The experiment claims to evaluate "unsupervised" anomaly detection, but then states that fault labels from maintenance logs exist and are used for evaluation. If labeled fault data exists, the correct framing is semi-supervised or supervised anomaly detection — the unsupervised label is misleading and carries design implications. True unsupervised anomaly detection does not use labels at any stage, including evaluation; using labels for evaluation but not training is semi-supervised, and the design should acknowledge and justify this choice explicitly. More importantly, the training-evaluation asymmetry means the autoencoder is trained without knowing what constitutes a fault, while the threshold-based baseline may have been tuned with domain expert knowledge of fault signatures. This creates an implicit asymmetry favoring the threshold system if it was calibrated on historical fault data.

Mean time to failure (MTTF) as the primary evaluation metric for an anomaly detection model is incoherent. MTTF measures the average time until a failure occurs — it is a property of the equipment or the failure process, not of the detection system's performance. What the design presumably means is "mean time from first anomaly alert to actual failure" (mean lead time), which is a different quantity. A model that never raises an alarm has infinite MTTF (no false alarms, but no detection either); a model that raises constant alarms would have a very short mean-lead-time but at enormous false positive cost. Neither case reflects a meaningful evaluation of detection quality. The correct metric is a function of both detection rate and false alarm rate — precision-recall at a fixed threshold, or F1 at the operational operating point.

The stratified random split applied to 12 months of streaming sensor data has the same leakage problem as other cases: equipment degradation sequences span weeks to months, so random split places observations from the same degradation trajectory into training and test. For an autoencoder trained to reconstruct "normal" behavior, this means the reconstruction error on held-out test observations from a known degradation sequence will be elevated in a way that reflects trajectory-specific abnormality — not the model's general ability to detect novel faults.

The autoencoder requires defining a reconstruction error threshold for fault detection. This threshold is a hyperparameter that must be tuned on the validation set. The design does not discuss threshold selection, which is the most consequential design choice for binary fault classification from reconstruction error. Different threshold choices can move precision-recall substantially, and reporting MTTF hides the threshold dependency entirely.

Stratifying on "time of day and operating conditions" as a confound control is insufficient for industrial equipment that may exhibit different failure modes under different operating regimes (high-load vs. low-load, startup vs. steady-state). The stratification treats operating conditions as a nuisance variable when they may be a moderating variable — the model may detect faults well under high-load but poorly under low-load, and averaging over these conditions in the primary metric hides regime-specific performance.
defender_raw:
The unsupervised anomaly detection design for industrial IoT predictive maintenance is motivated in its approach and honest about its evaluation challenges, with the MTTF metric and stratified split being the key open questions.

Autoencoder-based anomaly detection is appropriate for the unsupervised setting. The design correctly frames this as an unsupervised problem: labeled failure data is rare, and learning a normal-behavior reconstruction model that flags high reconstruction error as anomalous does not require failure labels during training. This is the standard and appropriate approach for sparse-failure industrial IoT settings.

The stratified random split to ensure fault representation across training, validation, and test sets addresses a genuine concern: if faults are extremely rare (1% or less), random splits could produce test sets with no fault events. Stratification ensures evaluable test performance. The temporal concern remains — degradation patterns develop sequentially — but the fault-representation problem is real and stratification addresses it.

Mean time to failure (MTTF) as the primary metric directly operationalizes the business value of early detection. A model that detects anomalies 4 hours earlier than the threshold baseline directly translates to 4 more hours of lead time for maintenance scheduling, which has quantifiable operational value. This metric is more interpretable to operations stakeholders than AUC or F1.

Precision at top-K anomalies as a secondary metric is appropriate for characterizing the quality of the anomaly ranking. In practice, maintenance teams respond to a queue of anomaly alerts; precision at the top K alerts determines how many responses are productive versus wasted.

Stratification by time of day and operating conditions as a confound control is appropriate. Industrial equipment exhibits different baseline sensor patterns under different operating modes, and a model that cannot distinguish operating-mode variation from degradation will generate false alarms during mode transitions.

The test set is reserved for final evaluation only, correctly implementing the evaluation discipline needed for an unsupervised model where threshold selection on the validation set could otherwise absorb all available signal.
all_issues_raised: The experiment claims unsupervised detection but uses fault labels for evaluation — this is semi-supervised at minimum; the framing creates an asymmetry where the autoencoder trains without fault knowledge while the threshold baseline may have been calibrated on historical faults.
Mean time to failure is a property of the equipment, not the detection system; the metric is incoherent as stated — a model that never alarms has infinite MTTF, not better performance.

Stratified random split on 12 months of streaming sensor data places observations from continuous degradation trajectories into both train and test, causing leakage in reconstruction-error-based anomaly detection.

The autoencoder reconstruction error threshold for fault classification is unspecified and is the most consequential hyperparameter; reporting MTTF rather than threshold-conditional precision-recall hides this dependency entirely.

Operating regime stratification treats load conditions as a nuisance variable when they may be a moderating variable — fault detection performance may differ fundamentally across operating regimes, and averaging over them in MTTF conceals this.
all_issues_adjudicated: The experiment claims unsupervised detection but uses fault labels for evaluation — this is semi-supervised at minimum; the framing creates an asymmetry where the autoencoder trains without fault knowledge while the threshold baseline may have been calibrated on historical faults.
Mean time to failure is a property of the equipment, not the detection system; the metric is incoherent as stated — a model that never alarms has infinite MTTF, not better performance.

Stratified random split on 12 months of streaming sensor data places observations from continuous degradation trajectories into both train and test, causing leakage in reconstruction-error-based anomaly detection.

The autoencoder reconstruction error threshold for fault classification is unspecified and is the most consequential hyperparameter; reporting MTTF rather than threshold-conditional precision-recall hides this dependency entirely.
